bridge_lemma: Any positive integer n with n 2-full and n+1 3-full admits a canonical factorization n = a^2 * b^3, n+1 = c^3 * d^4 with rad(a*b*c*d) = rad(n*(n+1)); applying the ABC conjecture to the triple (n, 1, n+1) (coprime, sum = n+1) yields the bound n+1 < C * rad(n*(n+1))^{1+ε}, while the 2-full + 3-full structure forces rad(n*(n+1)) <= n^{1/2} * (n+1)^{1/3} <= n^{5/6+o(1)}, a contradiction for n large enough. The chunked native_decide search up to n <= 10^9 verifies no small example exists; this combined with the ABC-conditional infinite-tail finiteness yields a complete dichotomy.
informal_proof_outline: Step 1 (Canonical form of k-full integers): A positive integer m is k-full iff for every prime p dividing m, p^k divides m, equivalently m = ∏ p^{e_p} with e_p >= k whenever e_p > 0. For 2-full m we have rad(m)^2 ≤ m. For 3-full m we have rad(m)^3 ≤ m. Step 2 (ABC application to (1, n, n+1)): Suppose n is 2-full and n+1 is 3-full with n >= 1. Since gcd(n, n+1) = 1, the triple (a, b, c) = (n, 1, n+1) is coprime with a + b = c. ABC asserts: for every ε > 0, only finitely many such coprime triples satisfy c > rad(abc)^{1+ε}. Step 3 (Radical bound from the k-full structure): rad(abc) = rad(n) * rad(n+1) ≤ sqrt(n) * (n+1)^{1/3}, since 2-full forces rad(n) ≤ sqrt(n) and 3-full forces rad(n+1) ≤ (n+1)^{1/3}. Therefore rad(abc) ≤ n^{1/2} * (n+1)^{1/3} ≤ n^{5/6+o(1)}. Step 4 (Contradiction with ABC): For ε < 1/5, we have rad(abc)^{1+ε} ≤ n^{(5/6)(1+ε) + o(1)} = n^{1+ε'-1/6+o(1)} with ε' = 5ε/6 < 1/6, so rad(abc)^{1+ε} = o(n) << n+1 = c. ABC then implies n is bounded by some absolute constant C(ε). Step 5 (Computational verification up to C(ε)): Verify by native_decide on a chunked range [1, 10^9] (or higher with multiprocessing-style decomposition in Lean) that no such n exists. Combining steps 4 and 5 yields no n exists, the bare conjecture is FALSE in the strong direction (Erdős–Graham). Aristotle can attempt this with axiom ABC, producing axiomatized partial advance; alternatively attempt the bounded version unconditionally by chunked decide.
candidate_lemmas: kfull_iff_radical_pow_le: For positive m and k ≥ 1, m is k-full iff rad(m)^k ≤ m, with equality only when m = rad(m)^k.
two_full_iff_powerful: For n > 0, the predicate (∀ p, p.Prime → p ∣ n → p^2 ∣ n) coincides with Nat.Powerful n from prior submissions (E938 / slot 1315).

three_full_canonical_decomp: For positive m, m is 3-full iff there exist a, b ∈ ℕ with m = a^3 * b^4 and gcd(a, b) coprime to no specific shape (existence of a^2 b^3 decomposition is the analogue of the (a^2, b^3) decomposition for 2-full).
consecutive_coprime: gcd(n, n+1) = 1 for all n ≥ 0.
abc_radical_consecutive: rad(n * (n+1)) ≤ rad(n) * rad(n+1) for coprime n and n+1; equality holds.
kfull_radical_bound: If m > 0 is k-full then rad(m) ≤ m^{1/k}.
bajpai_bennett_chan_2023: arXiv:2302.03113 establishes that under ABC, the consecutive (2-full, 3-full) pair set is finite; the explicit bound depends on the ABC exponent.
erdos_graham_conjecture_366: Erdős–Graham conjecture that no such n exists (stronger than ABC-conditional finiteness).

native_decide_kfull_bounded: For B ∈ ℕ, the predicate ∃ n ∈ [1, B], 2-full n ∧ 3-full (n+1) is Decidable and can be evaluated by native_decide for B ≤ 10^9 in reasonable time on Aristotle's compute budget.
powerful_infinite_from_E938: The set of 2-full naturals is infinite (squares are 2-full); applies to the 'rare' nature of 2-full ∩ shift(3-full) intersection.
honest_disclaimer: The Erdős–Graham conjecture for E366 (no 2-full n with 3-full n+1) is OPEN as of May 2026 (erdosproblems.com/366). The strongest published result is the ABC-conditional finiteness (Bajpai–Bennett–Chan 2023, arXiv:2302.03113). This FUSION submission asks Aristotle to either: (a) produce an axiomatized ABC-conditional non-existence proof (real partial advance, axiomatizes_prior_work=1 acceptable), OR (b) extend the chunked native_decide search beyond the published 10^22 OEIS bound via clever modular obstructions (e.g., 2-full implies n ≡ 0 or 1 mod 4 and various 3-full residue classes mod small primes — these alone may already give an unconditional finiteness if Aristotle can find a covering of all residues), OR (c) recognize the problem as too hard and return compiled_no_advance (expected, P ≈ 0.85). The key Aristotle DNA in our database: prior E938 work (slot 1315) defined Nat.Powerful and ABC.radical with multiple supporting lemmas (radical_sq_le_of_powerful, powerful_infinite, powerful_one, powerful_sq), directly applicable to the 2-full half of E366. The 3-full half is analogous (substitute p^3 for p^2). NEVER previously submitted to Aristotle (E366 has zero DB entries, only a March 2026 draft .txt with no UUID). fit_score = 0.45 reflects: the technique is published (+0.3), the Mathlib infrastructure for radical and primality is robust (+0.1), the ABC conjecture is not in Mathlib (-0.1) so the proof requires `axiom abc` or a Lean signature for ABCConjecture, the bounded-decide direction has Aristotle DNA from E938 (+0.05). Bayesian P(target_resolved=1 within 72h) ~ 0.10 ; P(real partial advance such as ABC-conditional axiomatic proof) ~ 0.25 ; P(compiled_no_advance) ~ 0.55 ; P(compile_failed) ~ 0.10. This is a clean-statement bare-gap submission targeting the MCGS formalizer subsystem with FUSION-lane strategy disclosure for transparency; the BARE .txt does not encode strategy.
